stimulus: Sculptor and ceramicist Toshiko Takaezu (1922–2011) is recognized for using her ceramic vessels and bronzes to evoke the natural world. In a paper for an art history class, a student claims that Takaezu had a distinctive gift for fusing techniques and influences drawn from different artistic traditions in a single work.
stem: Which quotation from a scholar describing Takaezu’s work would best support the student’s claim?

answerOptions.content: “In her closed-form vessel Mountain Memory, Takaezu joined a Japanese tea-ceremony glazing technique with the layered chromatic logic of American Abstract Expressionist painters such as Mark Rothko.”
“In her series Star Pillars, Takaezu arranged tall, sealed ceramic columns in groups across a gallery floor, inviting viewers to walk among them.”
“Made in the 1990s, Takaezu’s bronze bell Sound Form, cast to a height of roughly four feet, remains a touchstone of her late career and continues to attract scholarly attention today.”
“A particular group of Takaezu’s smaller works, produced after she returned from Japan, was thrown on the potter’s wheel and depicts forms inspired by ripening fruit.”

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because it presents a quotation directly supporting the student’s claim that Takaezu fused techniques and influences from different traditions in a single work. The quotation explains that Mountain Memory combines a Japanese tea-ceremony glazing technique with the chromatic strategies of American Abstract Expressionism. Choice B is incorrect because it discusses arrangement of works in a gallery, not the fusion of distinct traditions or techniques. Choice C is incorrect because it describes the bell’s dimensions and reputation but does not identify any fusion of traditions or techniques. Choice D is incorrect because the quotation describes throwing technique and subject matter but does not refer to multiple distinct artistic traditions being fused.
